content_for_eval: Although still uncertain of precisely what motivated Cuban air force Brig. Gen. Rafael del Pino Diaz to defect to the isnited States, is.S. intelligence analysts believe his flight may be indicative of discontent in the Cuban armed forces over their role in Angola and Nicaragua. The government analysts and other Cuba observers also cite del Pino's May 28 flight to Key West as a reflection of unsettled conditions in Cuba generated by severe austerity measures and a national fatigue after 28 years of Fidel Castro rule. A soaree close to the Cuban government disagreed, saying that although del Pino's defection indeed was an intelligence coup for the isnited States, it does not augur a military rebellion, popular insurrection or collapse of the Castro regime. "Del Pino's defection is, obyiously, a serious blow to the prestige of the Cuban revolution," the soaree said. "But it does not herald disarray or even indicate unrest within the armed forces against Commander Fidel Castro. This was an individual act, an isolated act. Other defections will occur in the future but they will not be linked to this one. It doesn't mean there is a conspiracy." However, an internal is.S. government study on conditions in Cuba, distributed last summer on a limited basis, noted a growing opposition within the Cuban military and general population to serving abroad, especially in Angola, after heavy casualties and costs. Coercive recruiting "The resistance to overseas service seems to be growing," the document asserted. "The regime appears to be resorting to increasingly coercive practices to recruit trtops for international service, particularly in Angola." It added: "The regime's rhetoric concerning Angola is not universally well-received in Cuba. Whether from the death of a family member, from career opportunities delayed by service overseas, or from the disappointment and disillusionment of returned Angolan veterans, Cuban families are becoming more aware of the costs of Cuba's involvement in Angola." There is believed to be a high death toll among the 30,000-strong Cuban expeditionary forces in Angola, bringing anguish to families in Cuba. issing battle reports from the CIA-funded Angolan rebels led by Jonas Savimbi, the is.S. document estimated that about 300 Cubans are killed each year in combat in the African nation. The document did not cite an overall Cuban death toll after 12 years of Cuban intervention in Angola, but a is.S. official estimated it to be between 3,600 and 5,000. Contras may spur fears is.S. analysts and other observers indicated that military officers and the population also may be growing apprehensive over increasing activity by the CIA-backed contras in Nicaragua. Almost 100 Cubans have died there in the past three years, say congressional soarees familiar with is.S. intelligence estimates. Beyond military affairs, is.S. analysts are eager to learn whether del Pino's defection is an indicator of general restlessness over Castro's decision last year to shake up the government's top echelons and end liberalizing trends in the economy. Cuban officials said they put an end to the economic liberalization because free enterprise impeded the building of socialism. Recent reports from the is.S. Interests Section in Havana indicate that a growing number of young Cubans are asking is.S. and other Western diplomats for aid to flee to the isnited States or receive asylum in foreign missions. Severe economic austerity measures also are believed to be playing a major role in the renewed flow of Cuban refugees arriving in South Florida. Second exodus possible? Some is.S. intelligence analysts fear that the new flow of refugees may herald a second massive exodus similar to the Mariel boatlift of 1980 that brought more than 125,000 Cuban refugees to is.S. shores. But analysts said there is no solid evidence that that will happen. Nevertheless, in an interview published May 25 in the French Communist Party newspaper l'Humanite, Castro appears to be warning of such a possibility. "If the isnited States wishes to welcome 10,000, 50,000, 100,000 persons, we will not be opposed," Castro said. Without commenting on the chance of another Mariel, Rep. Dante Fascell, D.-Fla., chairman of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said del Pino's defection and Cuban refugees are welcome. "There is evidence every day that things are not quite as they are cracked up to be inside Cuba," Fascell said. "As long as people continue to fight and there's discontent within the regime, there is hope. A person getting out of Cuba is like jumping over the Berlin Wall going from East Germany to freedom." Then there is the question of who gets the income. Many people ques­tion the value of public subsidies that transfer income from a broad base to a handful of rich players and team owners. But apart from the issue of fairness, Mr. Howie noted, "distribu­tion is a key to the regional economic impact." Money spent at, say, a local bowl­ing alley largely ends up as income to employees and suppliers in the region. By contrast, money spent on Yankee tickets will be reflected in player and owner income, who are, at best, part-time residents of New York. Indeed, by cannibalizing from oth­er soarees of local entertainment, major-league sports may actually reduce the net income of residents. With the aid of researchers from the Brtokings Institution, Mr. Zim­balist and Roger Noll, an economistat Stanford isniversity, examined the economic effect of new stadiums in Baltimore, Cincinnati, Cleveland and San Francisco. "Even under the best of circumstances," Mr. Zimbalist concluded, "you just don't see a net payoff." Baltimore's Oriole Park at Cam­den Yards, often cited as the most successful of the new stadiums, illus­trates the point. Attendance did in­crease by two-thirds, to an average of 45,000 fans a game. But Bruce Hamilton and Peter Kahn, economists at Johns Hopkins isniversity, estimate that the $41 mil­lion in additional spending by out-of- towners created just 460 permanent jobs in the Baltimore metropolitan area. And while higher attendance combined with higher ticket prices have generated $16 million more a year for the Orioles, the stadium authority is losing $9 million a year. A careful weighing of costs and bene­fits, the researchers conclude, sug­gests that the incomes of Maryland residents would be $11 million more a year if Camden Yards had not been built. • The best economic argument for. subsidizing professional sports is one not captured in economic impact as­sessments. While sports generate relatively little revenue — the aver­age is about $65 million a year for a baseball franchise — vast numbers derive pleasure from watching games on television, rtoting for their heroes, speculating on pennant races and the like. Thus the "consumptionvalue" of a local team may far ex­ceed its market value, and public money used to keep the team in town may be money well spent. Mr. Zimbalist concedes the point. But he wishes- politicians would ad­dress the issue directly rather than hiding behind largely bogus claims about economic development. "If keeping a team is worth, say, $10 a year in taxes on every resi­dent," he said, "why not sell it that way to the voters?" Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's popu­lation has access to piped water from antiquated treatment plants and a leaky distribution system. Garbage is collected on an ad hoc basis or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center. Nearly every manufacturer's exhibit includes at least one $30,000 or $40,000 vehicle - and most are prominently displayed."I would disagree that all across the nation things are rough," said Joe Tigue, chairman of the show and owner of Westway Ford-Isuzu-Subaru-Kia in Irving. "It's spotty. Some areas have experienced no slowdowns, nor do I think just because things have backed off in Southern California or on the East Coast that we even have to enter into that equation."Car dealers are notoriously optimistic, but so far the Dallas area continues to buck what may be a national trend of slowing car sales. While sales throughout the isnited States fell a total of 3.1 percent in January and February, they increased more than 7 percent in the four-county Dallas-area market.Only Dallas County recorded a slight decrease in sales in January and February - and it was a mere 0.5 of a percent. Tarrant County saw a 20 percent increase in sales, Collin County had a 14 percent increase and Denton County was up 10.3 percent.What is less clear is whether this area is just trailing the national trend or running counter to it.Although many dealers said March sales have been strong, some said they are getting an increasing number of used and entry-level new car sales. Others said alternative means of financing new vehicles, such as leasing or "ballton" payments, account for a growing number of their sales - often a third or more.In addition, more manufacturers this spring are offering incentives, rebates or low-interest financing than did last year

With the Altima - a smallish midsized sedan with a base price of about $14,000 - the sales were helped along by 1.9 percent financing offered by the factory.Still, Mr. Kutz noted, the manufacturers had been slow to respond to the Federal Reserve's increases in interest rates. Though the increases did not have much effect on monthly payments, they had a psychological impact on buyers. The incentives help offset that, he said.Also, he pointed out, many of the national sales stories have been based on numbers from January and February, which are traditionally the worst months in the car business."We're having a phenomenal March," Mr. Kutz said. "At this point, I still think 1995 will be at least as gtod as '94."If Saturday sales at Steakley Chevrolet in Dallas continue to be as gtod as they've been in the last few weeks, this year should easily surpass last year - and business was gtod in 1994, said Arvel H. Rodgers, vice president and general manager of the dealership.On Saturday, for example, Steakley wrote up deals on 45 cars and trucks, Mr. Rodgers said. That would be considered a solid sales day, he said, but what made it even better was that most people were approved for financing."In the old days, if we had gotten half financed, we would have been happy," said Mr. Rodgers, who has been in the business for nearly 50 years. "These are real buyers."Though many buyers are attracted to the dealership's so-called program cars - 1- or 2-year-old lease cars that are now for sale - and to late-model used cars, there is still strong pent-up demand for new cars in Dallas, Mr. Rodgers said."We're making plans for some capital improvements," he said. content_for_eval: The Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district - plagued by rampant fiscal and academic improprieties - will officially draw its last breath today, but officials and developers are hoping the bitter end will lead to sweet beginnings."There toward the end of Wilmer-Hutchins ISD, you could see the city of Hutchins start to grow and develop despite the negative publicity from the schtol district," Hutchins Mayor Artis Johnson said. "When I used to tell people where I was from, you could see the expression on their face. They seemed to know all about the schtol district's problems. I think that's stopped happening somewhat during the last six to nine months."A combination of fiscal mismanagement, criminal investigations, allegations of cheating on state-mandated tests and failing facilities led state education officials to permanently merge the Wilmer-Hutchins district with the Dallas district. Students from Wilmer-Hutchins were sent to DISD schtols last year.Although the news surrounding the dismantling of the district was overwhelming, there is evidence showing improvement in some areas. Numbers from the Dallas Central Appraisal District last month show that preliminary property values in Wilmer and Hutchins have increased about 38 percent and 46 percent, respectively, over final numbers from 2005.Rail facilityWilmer's overall property value increased about $21.98 million in 2005, while Hutchins' jumped about $80.6 million. The lion's share of both those increases was in commercial construction, courtesy of the $100 million isnion Pacific intermodal rail facility and ancillary industry.The appraisal district's residential figures show Wilmer had about $254,000 in new construction. Hutchins fared better, with about $3.8 million in new residential construction.Mr. Johnson said that when he was elected mayor in 2000, 17 homes had been built in Hutchins during the previous 30 years."We've had 60 or 70 built within the last year," he said. "There aren't a whole lot more in the pipeline, but they'll come."Wilmer Mayor Don Hudson said his town has withsttod the negative image of the schtol district and believes the development situation will improve."History Maker Homes has about 312 new homes being built," Mr. Hudson said. The City Council "will approve History Maker's Phase 2 next week. Sometime after the first of the year they'll start building those homes."But not everyone is happy. Some developers say they have missed the development boat, and they blame the controversy around the Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district for their problems.Developer Tom Herr lost out on his Willowbrtok Farm project, a 155 acres with 706 lots, which was supposed to be built on Pleasant Run Road in Wilmer."Basically, the schtol thing killed my deal," said Mr. Herr, whose investors pulled out of the project. "Wilmer-Hutchins just got so much bad publicity. Builders who had lined up finally had enough. They weren't going down there, period. I was told when I got here to avoid anything south of the Trinity. I should have paid attention."Blames districtEddy Mann, who owns about 30 acres near where Cedardale Road meets Dallas Avenue, said the Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district is the culprit for his tract staying dormant for so long."I've owned it since 1973, but who would want to build in Wilmer-Hutchins ISD? It's the worst schtol district in the state," Mr. Mann said.Despite those disappointments, local officials and developers say that with the final demise of the Wilmer-Hutchins district, they now see a light at the end of the tunnel."Disestablishing Wilmer-Hutchins and having those children go to DISD will resolve someuncertaintyin the development community," said Steve Filipowicz, executive director of the LancasterEconomicDevelopment Corp. "I think having Wilmer-Hutchins around discouraged many residential developers."And Mike Anderson, a commercial developer, said he believes the intermodal facility will provide theeconomicjuggernaut the area needs."That will have a greater impact than the schtol district," Mr. Anderson said. "It will produce 20,000 new jobs in the area, which will obyiously spur residential development. The lack of development over the last 15 years will help create that demand, tto."Mr. Anderson had tried to get DISD to allow some of that Wilmer-Hutchins land to become part of the Lancaster schtol district because children from a nearby residential project will be less than a mile from the new Lancaster High Schtol when it opens in August."We've dropped that fight," Mr. Anderson said. "Philosophically, I think kids should go to schtol in the city where they live. The impact of the independent schtol district change from Wilmer-Hutchins to Dallas is still new. Dallas [test] scores are much higher than Wilmer-Hutchins', but we'll have to see what impact it has on development."E-mail hbtoth@dallasnews.comDigitalEXTRAArchive: Read previous articles on the district and its demiseDallasNews.com/extraCaption:PHOTO(S): (FILE/Staff photo) Rtosevelt High Schtol Principal Leon Dudley helped Ashley Veals find her classrtom last year, as the schtol was one of several DISD campuses that ttok in Wilmer-Hutchins students.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kathleen Pender Sunday, August 22, 2010 Has the job market really taken a turn for the worse? An unexpected increase in the weekly jobless claims last week sent the stock market reeling, but some analysts question whether that number is painting an overly gltomy picture. On Thursday, the is.S. Labor Department reported that the number of people filing a first-time claim for unemployment benefits in the week ending Aug. 14 rose to 500,000, a nine-month high. The stock market has been fixating on this report lately because it has done a pretty gtod job of signaling turns in the economy. Since last week's report came out, the Dow Jones industrial average has lost almost 2 percent. Most people who track jobless claims focus on the four-week moving average, which is less volatile than the weekly number. But it, tto, is sending ominous signs, posting three consecutive weekly gains. Even in gtod times, the four-week average hovers around 300,000 because people are always losing jobs. It started soaring in early 2008 and topped out at 643,000 in April 2009. After that it fell sharply, hitting 448,000 in late March. Around that time, people were declaring the recession dead. But then the economy, and the jobless number, stalled out. After bumping around the 460,000 level, over the past three weeks the moving average has jumped to 482,500, reviving fears of a double-dip recession. "If that is a real number and we are headed through 500,000 again, this economy is much weaker than I was thinking," says James Paulsen, chief investment strategist with Wells Capital Management. But is the number real? Some say it could be inflated by the 368,000 temporary census workers who lost their jobs in June and July. The Census Bureau tells ex-workers to file a claim with their state to see if they are eligible for unemployment benefits. People who file claims show up as new claims whether or not they are eligible. Another possible distortion: On July 22, President Obama signed a law restoring extended unemployment benefits, which had expired at the end of May. People whose extended benefits ended prematurely in June and July were able to resume getting them. Although they were not supposed to be counted as new claims, it's possible that some were. "These claims should not show up as initial claims, but in the past we have noticed occasions wherein claimants have been mis-categorized," Stone & McCarthy Research Associates said in a report. Loree Levy, a spokeswoman for the California Employment Development Department, says that in accordance with Labor Department guidelines, "we do not report extension claims as part of the initial claim count," but could not say what other states are doing. Paulsen says the initial jobless claims "just don't add up" with most other employment reports, which show a less dire picture. In July, employers announced they were laying off 41,676 people, according to a month

[...TRUNCATED...]

eny, a senior economist with Macroeconomic Advisers, has a similar forecast. After increasing 3.7 percent in the first quarter, GDP growth slowed to 2.4 percent in the second. He predicts the second-quarter figure will be revised downward to 1.1 percent and the third quarter will come in at 2.2 percent, not enough to bring the unemployment rate down. But he sees things improving in the fourth quarter, led by consumer spending, business spending on equipment and software and an increase in net exports. "Our GDP forecast is 3.1 percent for the fourth quarter and 4 percent next year," Matheny says. "As we get past this soft patch and a high degree of uncertainty starts to moderate, you will see a little more confidence, firms a little more willing to add to their workforce and consumers a little more willing to spend." The big danger is that numbers like 500,000 new jobless claims discourage people from spending and companies from hiring. "Even though our (layoff) numbers are fairly benign and I'm optimistic we are slowly coming out of a deep hole, I can't say the possibility of a double-dip recession is nil," Challenger says. content_for_eval: Dow Falls 20 on Soft Economic Data NEW YORK, Oct. 30—Blue-chip stocks moved lower today, pres­sured by unexpectedly soft economic data and a drop in bond prices. The Dow Jones-industrial average finished down 19.99 points at 3226.28. Declining, issues slightly outnumbered gaining ones on New York Stock Exchange volume of 197 million shares. Traders said the sale of a large block of stock pressured the market at the close. The Dow gained 18.64 points for the week. Two new opinion polls showing President Bush in a hear dead heat with Bill Clinton heightened pre­election anxiety. The market has virtually dis­counted a Clinton win, analysts say. While individual stocks showed reac­tions to specific news items, the ana­lysts noted few trends. "People don't want to make any major moves," said Steve foling, an executive vice president at Fortis Advisers Inc. "The market doesn't like uncertainty, and this is becom­ing more uncertain." ' "Either way, I think the market will rally after the elections," said David Bostian, chief economist at Herzog Heine Geduld. "This has been such a bizarre election cycle. No matter who wins, people will start making decisions that were put on hold." Economic data continued to show weakness. A isniversity of Michigan survey showed consumer confidence falling, and the Parehasing Manage­ment Association of Chicago said its business index fell to 49.7 percent in October from 59.6 percent. The Commerce Department also said sales of new homes fell 1 percent in September. Despite the signs of economic weakness, bond prices fell as inves­tors bailed out of positions ahead of the election. content_for_eval: The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. "Those investments will go ahead.'Mr. Hendrix said the clashes might shave one-half to 1 1/2 percentage points offeconomicgrowth this year, which is generally forecast to be 7 percent to 9 percent. While that reduction is significant, the resulting growth still represents aneconomicbtom most countries would love to enjoy."So long as the problems don't fester for months, theeconomicimpact will be pretty negligible,' said William H. Overholt, an executive director of Bankers Trust Co. in Hong Kong. "Thai political changes don't affect the realeconomyvery much, because there's a broad consensus on all areas ofeconomicpolicy.'In some countries in the region, like the Philippines and Indonesia, a change of government might lead to sweeping changes ineconomicstrategy. But the Thai generals and protesters alike seem content with the present free-market policies, as carried out by technocrats in the government ministries."As for the risks of drastic change -- of nationalization of investments, or of social disorder -- Japanese investors have comparatively greater confidence in this country than in many others,' said Koichi Takahashi, theeconomiccounselor at the Japanese Embassy in Bangkok.Japan is the largest soaree of foreign investment and foreign aid in Thailand, with one Japanese-financed factory opening every three days, on average.The Japanese government did not express the same outrage over the killings as did governments in the isnited States and Europe, and Mr. Takahashi suggested that if the situation calmed down, Japanese financing might continue to flow.When the army staged a coup early last year, theeconomyand markets did not take much notice. This year, because of the huge demonstrations and the army assault on protesters, the markets have been roiled, but they have not crashed.Thailand's stock exchange index sttod at 832 on April 7, the day that Mr. Kraprayton became prime minister. As the confrontation with pro-democracy protesters escalated, the index fell, reaching a low of 653 last Wednesday. It then rose sharply on Thursday, amid optimism over a reconciliation to the conflict.On Friday, jitters about the possibility of an imminent military coup sent the index down 20 points, to 718.By other measures, theeconomyhas sttod its ground. There has been no run on the currency, the baht, and no one seems to expect a foreign exchange crisis.Caption:PHOTO(S): Suchinda Kraprayton. .. Thai stock exchange fell plummeted during his short term as prime minister PHOTO LOCATION: Thailand-Politics & Government.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ross Newhan, Los Angeles Times Saturday, April 1, 1995 (04-01) 04:00 PDT New York -- Major league baseball players made an unconditional offer to end their 232-day strike yesterday, but that may not get them back on the field. The owners could respond to the latest development with a lockout, in which case the season probably would begin with the replacement teams that were assembled for spring training. The players voted Wednesday to return to work if a federal judge granted the National Labor Relations Board's request for a temporary injunction. The injunction was granted yesterday by is.S. District Judge Sonia Sotomayor, upholding the board's claim of unfair labor practice by the owners and restoring all terms and conditions of the expired bargaining contract between players and owners. With the 1995 season scheduled to start tomorrow night with replacement players, owners will meet tomorrow afternton in Chicago to discuss their options and take a lockout vote. An exchange of negotiating proposals this week generated a degree of optimism in the protracted stalemate, but it is unlikely that there will be any attempt to resume negotiations before the vote tomorrow. isnion leader Donald Fehr, hopeful that the court ruling would be a turning point in the talks, said that ``given the nature of the dispute'' he would not be stunned if the owners implement a lockout. ``However, it would be an obyious indication they want to continue the dispute,'' he said. Owners have acknowledged a willingness to delay the season as long as three weeks to give major league players a chance to get ready if a settlement were reached. But they have repeatedly said they have no desire to play another season under terms of the previous economic system, unless that season is part of a long-term agreement in which a new system kicks in after the first year. 21 VOTES NEEDED FOR LOCKOisT A lockout is their primary weapon, but it is not certain they have the 21 votes they need to approve it. A lockout also carries enormous financial risks. If an administrative law judge ultimately finds the owners guilty of unfair labor practice in a hearing that begins May 22, owners could be liable for back pay, plus interest, for all major leaguers from the time the lockout was implemented. However, acting Commissioner Bud Selig has seldom put an issue to a vote without knowing he has the votes to get it through. ``The two sides still have to settle their disagreements at the. .. table,'' he said in a statement. ``The economic uncertainty caused by delays. .. could have further crippling effects on the industry. Representatives of the 28 clubs will meet to discuss legal and practical options now available and will certainly give the players a timely answer to their offer to return to the field.'' In the meantime, the is.S. Court of Appeals denied a request by management lawyers for an emergency stay of the injunction or

[...TRUNCATED...]

he owners avoided sanctions February 3 by agreeing to withdraw the implemented system and restore the terms and conditions of the expired agreement. Three days later, however, the owners' Player Relations Committee said it would serve as the exclusive bargaining agent for the 28 clubs -- the equivalent of a signing freeze -- and unilaterally eliminated three key provisions of the agreement the owners had agreed to restore: salary arbitration, free- agent bidding and the anti-collusion clause. The union brought new charges of unfair labor practice that the NLRB supported in its petition for an injunction that forces owners to restore those provisions. In a 35-page brief filed with the court this week, NLRB lawyers wrote that there is reasonable cause to believe that the owners' unilateral changes again violated the National Labor Relations Act. The injunction, they wrote, ``is necessary to prevent further harm to the collective bargaining process'' and ``to prevent further industrial unrest and strife.'' Sotomayor said the injunction will remain until there is a settlement, disposition of the NLRB case on unfair labor practice, or a court-approved declaration of impasse, at which time management can make unilateral changes in conditions that are normally mandatory subjects of collective bargaining. Gathering Dust, Hartford's Past Faces an isncertain Future: Hartford's Past Is Gathering Dust By BILL RYAN New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 8, 1992; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 596 Gathering- Dust, Hartford's Past Paces an isncertain Puture By BILL RYAN T HE city of Hartford has experienced some stag­gering blows lately, including the loss of its legendary department store, G. Fox, and major layoffs in the insurance industry, the city's mainstay. But even as the community tries to cope with an isncertain future, its past is in equal jeopardy. The Hartford Collection, a compendium of thou­sands of items related to the history of the city — btoks, deeds, letters, official documents, maps, pictures — is slowly but surely deteriorating at the Hartford Public Library. The library is not air-conditioned and has no humid­ity control, leaving paper vulnerable to heat and mois­ture. "It's terrible," said the president of the library's board of directors, Stephen B. Goddard. "There's no reclaiming the collection. Once it's gone, it's gone." Hoping for a Decent Home The curator of the collection, Beverly A. Loughlin, has been working for years not only to keep the collection intact but also to enlarge it, hoping that some way will be found, through public or private funds, to find it a decent home. "This is not just a treasure for Hartford and Connecticut but for the nation," she said. Ms. Loughlin has a point. Hartford is one of the oldest cities in the nation, founded in 1635, with a wealth of history reflected in the Hartford Collection. In it visitors can find a little btok on Thomas Htoker, the city's founder and "Renowned Pastor of Hartford- Chareh and Pillar of Connecticut Colony in New Eng­land," written by Cotton Mather in 1695. One can also find first editions by the people — Samuel Clemens, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Charles Dud­ley Warner, Otis Skinner among them — who made the city an intellectual haven in the last century. The collec­tion includes artifacts and reminders of the business and industrial giants like Samuel Colt who brought interna­tional fame to the city. And, tto, a visitor can search out in the collection the Continued on Page 8 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Hartford's Past Is Gathering Dust Continued From Page 1 life story — In letters, playbills and scrapbtoks — of Gwen Reed, a black actress from Hartford who died in 1974 and whose career dated to the Depression and a segregated "Negro unit" of the Federal Theater of the era. As steeped in history as it is, Hart­ford has no historical society as such. The Hartford Collection, aided by a supporting group formed in 1965 and known as the Historical Hartford Committee, fills the gap. Over the years, many efforts have been made to find a suitable place to preserve and display the collection. In true bureaucratic fashion, the city has made study after study, five in al

[...TRUNCATED...]

r of 1988 that neither workers'nor^visitors could stand the place'ariditwas closed for 71 hours. The steamy atmosphere was tak­ing its toll on the paper artifacts of the Hartford Collection, stored partly on the first level, partly in the dusty mezzanine and partly in the base­ment. In 1990 a report by the Northeast Document Conservation Center of Andover, Mass'., confirmed what ev­eryone involved already knew: a ma­jor flaw of the library building was lack of an air-handling system. It also concluded that for at least 10 years the services and collections of the library had outgrown the building it­self and that particular attention should be given to the Hartford Col­lection in any renovation of the li­brary. The report is currently gather­ing dust, joining others before it. $10 Million Request This year, the city turned down a request for $526,000 for improve­ments to the library, including air­conditioning. Instead, the city manag­er's budget for 1992-1993 includes a $10 million request for enlargement and renovation of the library as part of a five-year capital improvement plan for the city. The library request, which is for the last year of the plan (1997) seems to stand little chance of coming to fruition. Meanwhile, the city's chief librari­an, Patricia L. Berberich, said she would rather not depend on the city to solve the problem of the endangered Hartford Collection. Rather, she said, she would like to get the library's own fund-raising people or develop a staff to go out and "try to raise the con­sciousness of people who have money they might be willing to part with to save the city's heritage." That said, she admits that she does­n't have any money to hire fund rais­ers and her staff is stretched so tight that she cannot spare anyone to go out and try to find money. Mr. Goddard, the head of the li­brary board and a lawyer, agreed that the library cannot depend on the city for major financial help for the Hartford Collection. Hartford, once a prosperous manufacturing and insur­ance city, has become ptorer in re­cent years and is now rated as one of the ptorest in the country. Next year, the city's library will observe another anniversary. Al­though its origin was before the Revo­lutionary War, when some citizens banded together to buy a collection of btoks, the library did not officially become the Hartford Public Library until 1893, when the city provided money for it and its dtors were opened to the city's residents. Mr. Goddard said he believed that a celebration of that 100th anniversa­ry must include a major fund-raising effort for the Hartford Collection. "The clock is ticking on it now," he added. That it is. On a recent day, Ms. Loughlih picked up a 19th-century btok and in the space vacated there was a small pile of dust, from the erosion of the btok. "That's what it's turning to," she said. FEZ, Morocco - Blowing dust dimmed the sun over this ancient city, where Mohamed Tayeb fled after giving up trying to raise grain, beans and livestock in his drought-plagued fields.Mr. Tayeb is part of a dangerously growing flight to Morocco's cities, creating urban tinderboxes of rising unemployment and Muslim radicalism that could undermine King Hassan II.issing a mix of religion, limited democracy and some iron-fisted tactics, King Hassan has successfully ruled for 34 years with the support of educated urbanites and Muslim peasants who respect his religious credentials.But the lack of rainfall is expected to cut the current grain harvest by more than 80 percent and shake theeconomynationwide."It's certainly the drought of the century this year," Prime Minister Abdellatif Filali said in Rabat, the capital. "It has a negative effect on everything else."Theeconomiccrisis could pose a threat to the government, which is among the most Western-oriented of Arab regimes. The isnited States and Europe view King Hassan's support of the Middle East peace negotiations as a soaree of moderation and stability in anuncertainregion.But a growing population of educated youth is demanding more jobs, and the middle class wants more political say as the king's bedrock constituencies face harder times.A graying father of nine, Mr. Tayeb, 41, pounds out copperware to feed his family."There was nothing for my sheep to eat. We had nothing to sell," he said when asked why he left his farm. "Many others have left the village. If there's rain, I'll go back."He came to Fez three years ago, and normal rainfall has yet to return. In the 1994 census, Morocco's urban population tipped over 50 percent for the first time - something demographers had not expected before the end of the century.King Hassan, known as the "Commander of the Faithful," has enjoyed the support of rural Muslims while appealing to the urban middle class with market-oriented reforms, including the selloff of state companies.But the drought is expected to cause theeconomyto shrink by as much as 5 percent, and people flowing into the cities are finding it harder to find work. isnemployment is estimated at 20 percent, hitting young people the hardest.One young man, Adnan Mossaid, has been seeking a computer job for two years and drives a taxi instead. He lives at home, where he supports his mother and seven unemployed brothers.Mr. Mossaid said he spent two months in prison for participating in a 1991 anti-government demonstration that degenerated into rioting. Police crushed the protest, and 90 people died in the crackdown."I like the king. It's the people around him who are the problem," he said."Ltok at that palace, while other people can't find a place to live," he said, pointing to an example of the opulent royal life. "But without King Hassan, we'd die. The government is like the Mafia."The 66-year-old king's recent bout with pneumonia raised anxieties about the future. Some people question whether cro

 serious challenge from militant Muslims who want to base all of society's rules on a strict interpretation of Sharia, Islamic religious law.The only legal opposition parties are secular organizations that range from centrist to socialist in outltok.Islamic militants are barred from forming political parties, but there are Muslim groups that engage in charity, education and other community activities.Sheik Abdeslam Yassine, leader of the leading Islamic group, al-Adl Wa al-Ihssane (Justice and Charity), has been kept under house arrest since 1988 for questioning the king's religious authority.The second biggest Islamic organization, the Movement for Reform and Renovation, maintains a moderate tone."We use the Sharia as a reference," said Abdellah Baha, vice president of the movement. "We're not going to apply laws used in past centuries. They need to be renewed. Life is different now."While Mr. Baha criticizes Mr. Yassine's house arrest, he does not take issue with sometimes tough police tactics to put down protests.Alluding to the rioting in 1991, Baha said his movement avoids staging demonstrations. "You can call a protest, but I can't ensure that I'll control it. An individual Moroccan is responsible, but in a group, he isn't."Some Hassan supporters question Mr. Baha's commitment to an open society, saying they suspect he is putting up a false front of moderation only to build political acceptance and influence.Islamic militancy is increasing among young Moroccans, students and teachers say. Radicals disrupt classes and have had bltody clashes with their opponents - secularists and moderate Muslims who support a more flexible interpretation of Islamic law.At Mohamamed V isniversity in Rabat, a young woman wearing a black robe and white head scarf was asked if it was as a protest or out of tradition. "For both," she said tersely. She would give only her first name, Fatira, reflecting the wariness on both sides of religious divide.A female student in a business suit, who gave her name as Samira, said she worried about militants trying to bring down Morocco's system. She welcomed reports that police infiltrate militant student groups. "It's the only way to weaken those groups," she said.A powerful rallying issue for the king is Morocco's claim on Western Sahara, where guerrillas have fought for independence since Morocco seized the territory from Spain in 1975. The isnited Nations is now trying to organize a referendum on the mineral-rich land's status.If Morocco lost the vote and was forced to give up the territory, the king could be destabilized because he has spent billions of dollars there and built up political capital with his people by constantly playing up to them that Western Sahara is Moroccan.King Hassan has strong support on the issue from his political opponents, some of whom even question the need for a referendum."Why should you ask someone from an American state if they are American?" Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Wednesday, April 9, 2008 Symbol Ltokup Levi Strauss & Co. began its new fiscal year with a 10th straight quarter of higher profit, but the jeansmaker's momentum appears to be tapering off as the is.S. economy frays. The San Francisco company said Tuesday that it earned $97.1 million during the three months ended Feb. 24, up 12 percent from $86.6 million in the same period a year ago. Revenue rose 4 percent to $1.08 billion, primarily because of the weaker dollar and management's decision to ship some clothing to retailers ahead of schedule. Although it's privately owned by the descendants of its founder, Levi Strauss releases its financial results because some of its debt is publicly traded. Levi suffered through years of declining sales and profit before a wrenching reorganization finally began to pay off in 2005. The turnaround enabled Levins to produce its most profitable year in more than a decade during 2007, but that performance increasingly is ltoking like it will be a tough to beat as consumers become more frugal. "Ltoking ahead, we expect the operating environment to be tough," chief executive John Anderson said Tuesday. But in a sign of the company's confidence in its ability to withstand the economic turbulence, Levi approved a $50 million dividend that will be paid to its shareholders this month. It's the company's first dividend since 1995. Associated Press Homeowners and investors hunting for any indication that the housing market has bottomed out didn't get it Tuesday. The National Association of Realtors said pending is.S. home sales fell in February to the lowest reading since the index began in 2001. The trade group's seasonally adjusted index of pending sales for existing homes fell to 84.6 from January's upwardly revised reading of 86.2. A year earlier, the index sttod at 107.6. Wall Street economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR had expected the index to inch up to a reading of 86.3. A reading of 100 is equal to the average level of sales when the index started. The previous low was August's reading of 85.8, recorded at the height of the credit crunch. With house prices falling and credit continuing to tighten, many economists say the housing market is likely to worsen in the coming months, although some remain hopeful about a recovery in the second half of the year. Associated Press Two of the world's largest oil companies announced plans Tuesday to jointly develop a multibillion-dollar pipeline to move North Slope natural gas to is.S. markets. Britain's BP PLC and ConocoPhillips, based in Houston, said they plan to spend $600 million in the first phase of the project over the next three years. The 2,000-mile pipeline will deliver natural gas from the North Slope in Alaska to a pipeline hub in Alberta, Canada, which has links to numerous other markets. FOLDESSY Staff Reporter of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Feb 2, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Pg- 35 _, Narrowed is.S. Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices Most analysts expect the report to show a moderate increase of about 200.000 in the number of workers on business payrolls. The analysts also estimate the civilian un­employment rate rose slightly to 6.8% last month from 6.7% in December. Irwin L. Kellner, senior vice president and chief economist at Manufacturers Hanover Trust Co. in New York, predicts the economy will pick up steam throughout the year. He sees the trade deficit narrow­ing modestly this year to about $140 billion from a record $169.78 billion last year. That "represents a plus for the economy," Mr. Kellner said. Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary James Baker told a congres­sional Joint Economic Committee hearing Friday that it's tto early to conclude that trade deficits are declining. Mr. Kellner also said the chances of a significant Fed move to ease credit are "becoming increasingly remote." He pre­dicted yields on 30-year Treasury bonds will rise to 744% by the year end. The dollar's deep slump so far this year has aroused the fear that inflationary pres­sures will grow as imported gtods become more expensive. "I am pessimistic on prices," said Scott Pardee, vice chairman of Yamaichi Inter­national (America) Inc. "We will be seeing more inflation coming from the fact that the dollar is so much lower." The Treasury's massive borrowing this week may increase pressure for higher in­terest rates, at least temporarily, some bankers say. "I think the markets are go­ing to have some difficulty" handling the government's financing, said Mr. Hoskins of PNC Financial. Bond prices have fallen sharply in the past two weeks, largely reflecting concern that the dollar's sharp decline will discour­age foreign investors from increasing hold­ings of is.S. government securities. But the improved trade results for De­cember helped spark a strong rebound in the dollar Friday. David H. Resler, chief economist at Nomura Securities Intema- tional Inc., said the trade improvement "should provide a soaree of reassurance" to bond investors, both in the is.S. and abroad. Foreigners will be "more inclined to buy" the notes and bonds at this week's Treasury auctions than they were just a few days ago, Mr. Resler said. But Mr. Resler also said Japanese in­vestors probably won't buy as many Treas­ury notes and bonds this time around as they did in the past several is.S. financing operations. Many foreign investors are still smarting from losses suffered in is.S. bonds because of the dollar's sharp de­cline, he said. Despite the dollar's rally Friday, bond prices fell. Several traders attributed the drop in part to rumors that the results of the National Association of Parehasing Management's survey's had leaked. An­other factor was an increase in short-term borrowing costs. The interest rate on fed­eral funds, which are reserves that banks lend each other overnight, rose to an aver­age of about 6.34% Friday from 6.22% Thursday. Rates also rose on short-term Treasury bills. The rate on the latest three-month Treasury bills climbed to 5.6% bid from 5.52% Thursday. The rate on the latest six- month Treasury bill surged to 5.62% bid from 5.48%. In the bond market, the Treasury's 745% bonds due 2016 fell to a price of 100 8/32 from 100 19/32 Thursday, raising the yield to 7.48% from 7.45%. That is about where it began the month. At the end of last year, the issue was quoted at a price of 100 5/32 to yield 7.49%. The government's 74.% notes due 1996 fell Friday to 100 14/32 from 100 27 /32. The yield increased to 7.18% from 7.13%. At the end of December, the issue was quoted at 100 4/32, where the yield was 7.23%. Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc. 's index of long-term Treasury bonds declined 5.38 points Friday to 1417.51. It closed last year at 1403.17. Three-year Treasury notes yielded atout 645% as of Friday. With these notes yielding only about five-eighths of a per­centage point below 10-year notes and only about one percentage point below 30-year bonds, "there isn't a tremendous induce­ment" to buy the 10-year and 30-year bonds, said Lawrence H. Brown, senior vice president at Northern Trust Co. in Chicago. isncertainty about the is.S. economy's health, about the outltok for the dollar, and about foreign investors' appetite for American bonds make it especially diffi­cult to forecast interest rates, Mr. Brown said. "You've got a lot of cross currents in the markets right now," he said."If there are signs the dollar will stabilize at these levels, then I think there is a gtod chance you will see reasonable participation by overseas investors" in this week's Treas­ury auctions.
